few weeks and then come back a few months later. However, if you experience diarrhea for an extended period of time, you should consider contacting a health care professional. Diarrhea can be a symptom of many diseases or conditions, so it's important to seek medical attention if you become concerned about diarrhea.

Diarrhea can happen because of many things, including:

Bacteria and viruses – Some bacteria can cause diarrhea, while others can lead to diarrhea. Viruses, such as the flu, can also cause diarrhea, though it's more common in children than adults.

Food allergies – Some people can't digest some foods, so food allergies can lead to an episode of diarrhoea.

Anemia – Anemia can lead to a loss of fluids, which can lead to dehydration. Anemia can also affect the way you pass urine, so it can be a common cause of diarrhoeah.

, chairman of Singapore's biggest food company, to ensure that the nation's food supply chain remains stable and secure.

"When you see an economic downturn, you don't think about the food supply chain," Mr Lim said at a press conference last week, at a time when food shortages are on the increase in many parts of the world, due to drought, flooding and food-borne diseases. "But in this country, because it's our national food, it's so important."

Mr Lim's concern for maintaining food supply chain stability comes at a time of uncertainty when the global food system is being threatened by drought, food-related diseases and a food price shock that has pushed some people into starvation.

The latest World Health Organisation figures show that the number of people suffering from food shortages worldwide has jumped from 7.8 billion people five years ago to an estimated 9.9 billion by 2020, with half the world now at risk of being food-starved.

United States has one of the worst maternal mortality rates in the world, according to the UN's World Health Organisation, at 41 deaths per 100,000 live births.
Maternal death rates are falling across many industrialised nations, but the United States has only managed to make minor improvements over decades, according to a recent review from the American Journal of Preventive Medicine and Maternal Mortality Review.
The review, published this week, found that maternal mortality rates improved significantly in the United Kingdom, Canada, New Zealand, Iceland, Sweden, South Africa, Australia and many others.
But in the United States, maternal mortality rates have only improved marginally over a period spanning decades, according Toomey said.
"This review highlights an important issue that we often don't talk about because it's a topic that's a little bit out of our comfort zones," Toomey, a professor at Harvard Medical School, said.

The findings, published today in the Astrophysical Journal, suggest that water could be found as a by-product of water-based life, and could provide a potential clue to whether a water-dependent world can sustain life on Earth-like planets, according to a press release.

But they’re also an important part of a landscape as part of a more-than-decorative garden in dry climates, where they provide year-round texture and a welcome moisture barrier to plants and soil.

Diascia are a great choice for gardeners looking to establish a tropical or subtropical garden in a dry climate, since they can grow in temperatures below freezing, making them an ideal plant to plant in pots that will freeze overnight.

They can also tolerate very low temperatures, so they make a good plant to plant near a heater, if you can afford it, to keep a garden warmer during colder periods.

When you plant a diaza, you don’t plant it directly into the ground; you plant it into a container, so it will grow up, rather than out, and you can plant it into sunny, well-drained soil, and it will grow and grow and grow in a short time.

The global temperature has risen by a record 2 degrees Celsius since pre-industrial times, while global GDP has continued to grow at a very low rate, said UNEP Executive Director Achim Steiner at a press conference held on Monday to present the report, "The Economics of a Green Economy."

"The economic crisis has led to a deep recession, while fossil-fuel-fired energy sources and energy-intensive industry continue to grow in importance," said Steiner. "Our global economy needs to shift from fossil fuels to a more energy-efficient, low-carbon one, to avert the worst consequences of climate change."

The report, which focuses on the economic implications of climate change, points out that energy efficiency, renewable energy and low-carbon infrastructure are central to a green economy, but these options "are only part of the solution."

Americans support a national health care plan, according to a new survey by a health care-focused think tank and a major health care research organization.
The Kaiser Family Foundation, a non-partisan group, asked its members whether they supported a national health plan that would include some form of health insurance coverage, a single-payer system, a hybrid system, a "public option" plan and a "private option."
The results were stark, with 71 percent saying they supported a single- payer system, while only 14 percent were opposed.
The survey, a follow-up to a survey of health care-related Americans taken last summer, showed that "the majority of Americans believe that a single payer system would improve the health care system and make it more affordable," said Karen Pollitz, director of the Kaiser Family Foundation's Kaiser Health Tracking Poll.
The results "suggest that Americans understand that a single-payer system will improve the health system and make health care more affordable,"

They wanted to make college admissions a more individualized process that gave students a chance to showcase why they were a great fit for a school, rather than a one-size fits all approach.

The problem, according to one former official at College Board, lies in the idea that colleges can measure a student's ability by a single factor that can't be changed, such as a good SAT or ACT scores, high school grades, or SAT or ACT composite scores. "The problem is that students can't change their scores, so that's a very hard factor to judge," said one former official, who spoke on the condition of anonymity, because of an ongoing court battle over intellectual property rights. "And it's very hard to judge a college based on a single factor."

The German Greens, the largest party, saw their support plummet, losing almost half their seats and finishing second in the polls behind the Social Democrats. They were only able to win 8 seats out of a total of 101, the party's worst result in any election since 1949.

Meanwhile, the German left-right coalition led by Chancellor Angela Merkel's Christian Democratic Union, or CDU, saw its support increase by five percent, making it the third-largest party in the European Parliament. However, it still fell behind the Social Democratic Party, which gained 20 percent of its own seats, winning 71, including one seat from Turkey, one from Greece, one from Italy, one from Luxembourg and one from Belgium.

But how can you tell that your $100 gift will go towards a good cause?

Well, if you can look at it objectively, you can see that a $100 gift is almost certainly going to result in a good result, according to a recent study out of Cornell University.

The Cornell study, published in the Journal of Applied Economics, examined whether a charitable donation of $100 will increase a recipient’s net worth by $100, $200, or $400 by taking into account a number of variables.

One of those variables, the value of a gift, was found to increase a recipient by an average of $3,200, $3,600, or $5,100. However, a $100 donation would only increase a recipient's net worth by an average $1,900, $3200, or only $1,000.

The state of Texas will no longer accept a federal lawsuit filed by a Dallas-area attorney that accuses a state prison system official of sexual abuse and torture in a Texas prison, according to a federal judge's decision Thursday.

The judge said the Texas Department of Criminal Justice has "effectively settled" one claim and that it will no longer defend itself on two other claims in the lawsuit, filed by attorney Gary R. Hirsch, a Dallas-based attorney, according to court documents.

Rajesh Patel, a spokesman for the Texas Department, said that the department will "vigorously defend against any of the claims."

"The department has filed a motion to dismiss and will continue to litigate, if necessary, to ensure that justice is served," Patel said in a written statement.

Patel said that Hirsch's claims were "unsupported by any of the factual allegations."

The economic contraction across the whole of North and Central Europe will lead to a lower-than-expected import and export volume,” according to a report by the German economic research body, IBD/TIPP, published Monday.
The German trade group said that it expected exports to decline by 2% and imports by 4%. While this forecast does not include the impact from the coronavirus, IBD noted that it will still result in a lower trade balance than last year and a lower surplus than in 2019.
The IBD/ TIPP trade balance, a measure of exports minus imports, fell from a surplus of EUR 9.9 billion ($11.5 billion) to a deficit of EUR 5 billion last year. It was seen falling to a surplus of between EUR 5 billion and EUR 7 billion this year, IBD said.

Greek philosophy to a modern audience, although he did not himself develop Greek philosophy. His writings are a collection of essays from a long career of teaching and writing, which is sometimes called a "biography" because it contains so many entries, rather than a "history." His writings include De philosophia, De philosophia et philosophia, and De philosophia and philosophia. His book De philosophia was written while he was living near Constantinople, a city that fell to the Persians in 480 B.C. His influence on the development of philosophy can be seen in the fact that Aristotle's De philosophia is based on several essays by Proclus, and that the book De philosophiae, written by Plato, is based on three essays written by Proclus and on two by Proclus's colleague, Demosthenes. Although Proclus is often credited with writing De philosophia himself, it is believed that Proclus did not actually write it, although it bears his name. Proclus

The Edinburgh event is taking place from December 2 until January 2, 2020.
The event will take place across the city, starting with a fireworks display on December 2, followed by a procession along the Royal Mile on December 3 and a fireworks show at Calton Hill on December 4.
A procession and fireworks will take place on New Year's Eve, followed by fireworks at Calton Hills on New Year’s Day, which will be followed by an ice rink at Murrayfield on December 30.
The Edinburgh Fireworks will finish the celebrations on January 1.
What is it?
The iconic Edinburgh Fireworks displays will bring to a close a hugely successful year, one that saw an average of over a million people enjoying the show.
The show has become an integral part of Scotland's festive season, attracting over one million people to the city every year and generating around £40million worth of trade and investment during 2018/2019.

The national unemployment rate rose from 7.7 percent to 7.8 percent, according to the Labor Department.

Unemployment rates were higher in 14 states, including Alaska, Arizona, California, Delaware, Florida, Georgia, Illinois, Indiana, Kansas, Kentucky, Louisiana, Mississippi, Missouri, Nebraska, North Carolina, Oklahoma, South Carolina, Tennessee, Texas, Washington, West Virginia, and Wyoming.

"Today's report suggests that the current economic recovery will continue to be a long one," said Gus Faucher, a senior economist at PNC Financial Services, in a research note. "The unemployment rate remains above its long-term average, suggesting that the recovery will continue, albeit at a slower pace, for several more months."

Unadjusted unemployment rates were higher across many economic sectors, though the rate for construction, a key indicator of the national economy, dropped significantly in October from 9.2 percent to 7 percent.

Halliburton, who served as a key prosecution witness, admitted to using illegal drugs and alcohol during an interview with investigators in 2005 and has since pleaded guilty to multiple drug-related crimes.

But an OJO review found that between 2002 and 2004, Halliburton's drug-related cases were prosecuted to a rate of about 60 percent, while cases that were tainted by drug use were prosecuted at about 40 percent, according to the OJO report. DUBAI (Reuters) - Iran will continue to enrich uranium to 20 percent purity, its nuclear chief said, as Tehran presses on with uranium production at a facility near the capital Tehran, a day after a nuclear deal with world powers expired.

Iran has said it will begin enriching uranium to 20-percent purity, its highest level ever, within a week, if it fails to win an extension to its 2015 nuclear pact, which President Donald Trump has threatened to quit unless it revises its policies.

Rouhani, speaking to reporters at a conference in Tehran, said Tehran was still waiting for any positive signal from world powers on extending an agreement that limits its nuclear program in exchange for the lifting of crippling economic sanctions.

“We hope that if necessary, an extension to the nuclear deal with the remaining powers will be given,” Rouhani said,

Bread for the World, a food pantry operated by the United Way of Greater Canton/Wayne County, will receive a $2,500 contribution from the Stark County Community Foundation to go toward holiday baskets to distribute to low-income families.

The funds will help the organization purchase turkeys, ham, cranberry sauce and sweet potatoes for families who might otherwise find it hard to afford those items this holiday season.

"For families that don't come into a lot of money, it's a big help," said Michelle Raucher, director of development at United Way of Stark County/Wayne, a division of United Way of Northeast Ohio, which oversees Bread for the World. "This will go a long way."

Raucher also said Bread for the Future will use the contribution to help cover expenses such as gas and travel, which can run between $100 and $150 per family, based on what families will be purchasing.

ORLANDO, Fla. (AP) — A judge acquitted the widow of the Pulse nightclub shooter of charges of obstruction and providing material support to a terrorist organization in the July 2016 shooting at the Orlando nightclub.

the climate crisis, warns the new report of the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) released on Monday.

The report calls for urgent, rapid and unprecedented action to prevent the climate crisis from becoming more severe and more frequent, and to limit global warming to under 2 degrees Celsius.

A 2-degree increase in global average temperature would increase the frequency, intensity and duration of extreme weather events, and lead to a rise in sea level of several metres.

The global average temperature has already increased by about 0.8 Celsius between 1880 and 2010.

And the world is on course for a temperature increase of 3 to 4 degrees Celsius by the end of the century.

For men with male pattern hair loss, it is now possible to restore the hair growth patterns of the past and to restore the density and appearance of their hair. Hair transplantation is a medical procedure that is used to provide an individual with hair loss by growing hair in areas of the scalp where there is insufficient hair.

Male pattern hair loss is a common condition that affects approximately 30 percent of men. It is the most common hair loss condition affecting men of all ages, and it is estimated that about half of men will have some degree of hair loss at some point in their life. Male pattern hair loss is also known as follicular or telogen effluvium, and it is a common condition among men who have had male circumcision. the atmosphere through human activities, it will warm the planet. That's a fact that is well-known to scientists, but how much warming will it produce?
A new study by a team of scientists at the University of Delaware and elsewhere in the U.S. and Canada suggests that in the absence of human emissions, temperatures would rise about 6 degrees Fahrenheit (3 degrees Celsius) by 2100, or about 3 degrees Celsius by the end of the 21st century.
The finding is in line with an earlier study by the same group published in the journal Science, but it is a much more dramatic increase than the one that has been predicted by climate models.
In the new study, the researchers found that the temperature increase could be even steeper if emissions are reduced, or even if they continue to increase.
"If we are serious about reducing carbon emissions, we need to act now," said co-author David Keith, a professor of atmospheric science at the University of Delaware.

consensus is that the banks will not see any significant impact from the Fed rate hike.

But the consensus is wrong.

The Fed’s decision to hike rates by 25 basis points on Oct. 26 will have some effect. It will push interest rates on mortgages and credit card loans higher, which will push the cost of borrowing higher. But that’s about it.

The biggest impact will come from the Fed’s decision to begin the process of unwinding its $4.5 trillion balance sheet. The central bank will start to shrink its balance sheet by selling investments, which will make those investments less attractive to banks and other investors, and it will also reduce the amount of cash it has sitting on its balance sheet. That means the economy will have a smaller pool of money to spend on loans to the real economy.

Hogmanay is a celebration of the end of the year that takes place in the Scottish capital, Edinburgh, Scotland. The festivities last throughout the evening from 5pm on the evening of Friday, December 31, until around midnight on Saturday, January 1. The celebrations take place in the city centre of the Scottish capital, Edinburgh.

, has been built on the premise that silicon is the future. Now, researchers at UC Berkeley have devised a way to make silicon-based transistors more energy-efficient, which could have a profound impact on the global economy as well as computing power.
The researchers, including Drs. Stephen Leone and Daniel Neumark, have developed a method called "metal-insulator-metal" (MIM) transistor design that could make more power-efficient chips. The idea is to make the metal in the channel of a MIM transistor smaller than the width of the silicon channel of a conventional transistor. The metal that makes up the channel is called the metal-insulator-metal gate (MIMG).
The researchers were able to make MIM transistors with reduced leakage current, and higher energy efficiency, by reducing the size of the MIMG.

But the discovery of a gene that appears to regulate the activity of a protein called PINK1 in the brains of elephants may offer new insights into the species' ability to fight cancer, researchers say.

The gene, called PINK1, has been found in a variety of animal and human cancers, but has been a mystery in elephants, which are not normally affected by tumors.

The scientists, from the University of California, San Francisco, and the Max Planck Institute of Molecular Cell Biology in Germany, recently found that the gene is active in the brains of African elephants, but is silenced in Asian elephants.

"This is the first time we've seen this gene and this protein in the same organism, and it's active in an animal that's not normally affected by cancer," said lead author Matthew Hwang, a professor in the Department of Molecular and Systems Biology at UCSF.

In his recent article, “We’re Not Done Reading the Book,” Peter Norvig makes the argument that reading is an activity that we do for pleasure and education. He goes on to argue that we should not only read for pleasure, but also for learning. Norvig suggests that we should use that learning to better understand how our world works.

Norvig points out that “our books contain all of the information that we need to understand the world and ourselves.” As such, he argues that we should use our time to read books for a deeper understanding.

But while Norvig is right that we should read for learning, he’s wrong when it comes to the kind of books that we should read. He argues that we should read “books that provide us with a rich and richly detailed experience of the world,” but he’s not exactly clear about what that means.
